caseStudies.description:
Preply is a global online language tutoring marketplace. I joined the Growth org as a contract Senior Product Designer, working mainly on the tutor side of the marketplace. Two primary projects: Editable Trial Agenda and the iOS tutor widget.

Alongside, I contributed to the Preply design system, used AI-coded prototypes for concept testing, partnered closely with DesignOps, UX Research, Data Analytics, and UX Writing, and pressure-tested direction in design jams and concept reviews with peers across teams.

sections.title: Editable Trial Agenda
sections.description:
The Trial Agenda is the screen tutors use during a trial lesson to drive subscription conversion. V1 worked everywhere except where it mattered most: Power Tutors — who deliver the majority of trials and drive CR2Sub — rejected it as too basic. V2 added scheduling prompts and was killed for flat conversion and a drop in monetization. One signal held across iterations: the agenda needed to belong to the tutor, not to Preply.

I joined Preply's Growth team as a contract Senior Product Designer and owned the project end-to-end — research brief, usability testing, design from concept through the Phase 1 / Phase 2 split with the PM. Bex, the senior designer who owned this design area, partnered through the work; direction was set jointly.

The bet was a contract change: the agenda stops being Preply's checklist and becomes the tutor's artifact — freshly generated each trial from what the platform already knew about the student, editable on the fly. A new "prepare-then-use" behavior emerged: tutors edit pre-class and reference live. Per-tutor defaults and a shareable-with-student hook surfaced in testing — both deferred to Phase 2 to keep Phase 1 small and shippable.

A/B across ~37K tutors. Stat-sig lifts across engagement and monetization. Shipped. 

**Detailed metrics available upon request.**

sections.title: Tutor iOS widget
sections.description:
The V1 widget shipped in Q3 2025 with two states — a non-inferiority bet by App Core to unlock iOS widget infrastructure for other product squads. It passed but didn't move business metrics. Field signals showed appetite: 3.5% organic adoption, 6% with prompt, tutor adoption above student.

PM Ariadna brought me the brief with four candidate states. I joined as a contract Senior Product Designer and owned the design end-to-end, refreshing Preply's internal iOS widget guidelines along the way.

I mapped the full tutor lifecycle — new, recently activated, active, mature — proposed a state taxonomy, and tied each state to a business signal: first-week churn, TWA, Super Tutor conversion, advocacy. PM partnered on refinement. Each state got its own widget URL so other squads could extend the surface. Phase 1 shipped the highest-leverage states; the rest held for Phase 2.

Four new widget surfaces shipped; adoption grew over the year post-launch. 

**Detailed metrics available upon request.**

caseStudies.description:
At Guesty, I worked across two product groups. In Business & Financials, I owned design for Additional Fees — from creation flow to how they surface across the platform — and co-led Mid-stay Relocation alongside Inesa Muchnik. In the Cross-platform team, I led the Notifications system rebuild and the Login & Registration flow revamp.

Alongside product work, I ran hiring interviews, mentored teammates, and regularly contributed to the design system.

sections.title: Notifications Revamp
sections.description:
Notifications were a silent operational risk. Everything landed in one flat, messy list — a reservation cancellation tomorrow next to one two weeks out, alongside payment failures, channel sync errors, and routine confirmations. Customers had built Zapier integrations to route around it. 75 PFRs from 113 accounts, 19 interviews, a 50-ticket CS audit — the problem was real and overdue.

A strategic call early on: build on a third-party notification platform instead of from scratch — accepting its API constraints in exchange for cutting time-to-market roughly in half, and treating this as Phase 1 of a 5-phase roadmap (customizable triggers, collaborative lifecycle, notifications SDK).

I led the project end-to-end — reshaping information architecture, auditing ~50 event types, benchmarking references, and designing the new system in alignment with the PM and key stakeholders: a severity taxonomy (Critical / Important / Updates), permission-aware preferences with cost-aware delivery defaults, and a scalable layout with Phase 2 designs prepped.

**Detailed metrics available upon request.**

sections.title: Mid-stay Relocation
sections.description:
Moving a guest between units mid-stay required a five-step workaround — dummy listings, shortened reservations, manual reconciliation, broken automations, a new confirmation code — a recurring operational tax with no clean path through the system.

A 0→1 feature spanning eight teams — one of the largest cross-team initiatives in Guesty's history. I co-led design with Inesa Muchnik, owning the core relocation flow: rate recalculation, owner revenue split, and in-modal pricing choice — all while keeping a single reservation ID intact across owner statements, channel sync, folio, and calendar.

The hard part wasn't the UI — mid-stay relocation served two distinct sets of jobs: operational (which property, which dates, how revenue splits) and financial (full folio breakdown with before-and-after across fees, taxes, line items). We split the modal accordingly — Timeline and Folio breakdown — so each user gets exactly what they need in their context.

**Detailed metrics available upon request.**

caseStudies.description:
I spent three years at SimilarWeb working across Core Platform Products — the navigation redesign, the Website Segments builder, and two new pods launched from scratch (Market Research and Demand Analysis). Across all of them, I worked closely with PMs and key stakeholders to shape product strategy and define design 
direction.

Alongside this, I pushed for system-level consistency, championed UX, set clearer cross-designer process, ran Figma sessions for engineers, and hired and mentored designers across teams.
caseStudies.scope: Product Strategy, Information Architecture,  Stakeholder Alignment, 0⇢1 Product Launch

sections.title: Navigation redesign
sections.description:
Six months after the previous redesign, the sidebar navigation was still broken: unaligned IA across product sections, users losing filters, inputs and context between pages, inconsistent visual hierarchy and patterns.

Bottom-up initiative. Started as a 1.5-hour design exercise, became a cross-team push we sold to stakeholders ourselves. I led discovery through delivery across 5 designers, 2 team leads, PMs, FE/BE, and UX writer.

The Acquisition homepage was the structural blocker. Its owners defended it until a 0.6% engagement number settled the argument. Removing it unlocked simplified IA across the entire platform.

**Nav time 1.3 min → 42 sec (-53%); MAU increased by 2-3X on buried pages; Median pageviews per session 4.5 → 5 (+10%).**

sections.title: Website Segments
sections.description:
Website Segments lets users group URLs by specific parameters to track any defined slices of sites separately. ~35% of platform revenue comes from this functionality, with pricing scaled by segment quota — even Top Tier Enterprise packages capped at 25 segments, so every new segment was direct revenue. 

Segment builder completion rate was stuck at 64% and CS managers were doing most of the creation for clients. In close collaboration with VP of Product and PM we defined three concrete blockers: opaque Include/Exclude logic, a multi-step wizard misaligned with users' mental models, and no final URL list preview. 

I owned the full redesign cycle from discovery to delivery. Single-screen builder, live URL list preview, two clear Include/Exclude buckets editable side-by-side. 

**Completion rate 64% → 81% (+16.8pp); Time to create 4 min 5 sec → 23 sec (-90.6%); Segments per user +0.633.**

sections.title: Market Research
sections.description:
Market Research launched in September 2023 as a new section in the platform — helping business leaders identify new markets, benchmark on KPIs, and size markets by sector and geography. Built to eventually replace the legacy Web Category Analysis surface, which had stalled on retention.

Fast 0-to-1 launch. We reused legacy data pipelines but rebuilt the UX from scratch — a new section with multiple pages (Overview, Segmentation, Players, Geography, Demographics) shipped as a paid add-on. I owned design end-to-end.

The design bet: surface preset answers for each business question instead of asking users to assemble them from filters and pickers. Players became the most-used surface in the section, and the new **Quadrant widget drew 2X the interaction rate vs the legacy one.** One Quadrant spotted in a blog article drove an $80K deal back from Wolverine.

**38% of accounts purchased the new add-on within 6 months; 63% of users visited Players page within day one, 58.8% returned 3+ times within a month; 4-week retention +18% vs legacy section.**

sections.title: Demand Analysis
sections.description:
Demand Analysis launched in February 2024 as a second new section in the platform, alongside Market Research. Focused on forward-looking demand signals — projecting where audience attention was moving, not just describing where it had been. 

I owned design end-to-end across Search Trends (the lead surface), period-over-period (PoP) mode, and the keyword list engine that powered the section. 

Working closely with PM and VP, we shaped the section's product strategy — and made the core bet on the keyword list generation mechanic. Previously a fully manual process — users uploaded CSVs or added keywords one by one. We rebuilt it so users could auto-generate an entire list from a single seed keyword, which then could be edited and saved. 

**Search Trends MAU: +70% vs old Traffic by Country page (by month 4); Save events on generated lists: +34%; Edit events on generated lists: +42%**

caseStudies.description:
Sber stood up an internal PFM (Personal Financial Management) tribe to address Russia's savings problem — 43% of adults had no savings, 82% didn't plan expenses, 98% didn't invest. The tribe scaled from five to twelve product teams over three years. 
I led design across the Total Funds group — owned the central screen solo and directed the group as it grew from one team to three.

Alongside the product work, I led hiring for the product group, mentored across the design team, and directly managed two designers. With colleagues from Sber, I also taught product design at the British Higher School of Art and Design (Moscow) — reviewing student work, and mentoring students into the industry.
caseStudies.scope: Product Strategy, Design Thinking, Information Architecture, Stakeholder Alignment

heroImage.alt: Total Funds redesign — old 12-segment product pie versus new asset-type donut with leading buttons

sections.title: Discovery
sections.description:
As Russia's largest bank, Sber could take this on at scale — both the social weight and a clear commercial upside. But the product itself was undefined: Total Funds was conceived as the group's hub, with its mental model, audience, and priorities all open.

Discovery was nonlinear — strategy sessions with PMs across teams, brainstorms with tribe leadership, repeated iteration on what "manage your money efficiently" should mean in product terms.

Formal research closed the phase: a 1,200-user quant study mapped four target audiences; a 6-day Design Thinking session with 13 respondents generated 145 ideas, 87 of which entered the backlog.

The output set direction for the Total Funds rebuild and seeded the backlog that grew into Financial Cushion, the analytical iteration, and adjacent products across the group.

images.alt: Discovery phase placeholder — to be replaced via the in-app editor

sections.title: Total Funds rebuild
sections.description:
The old Total Funds was a 12-segment pie nobody read as "composition of capital." Users couldn't tell what they owned, and the screen ended at the chart — no path to the next action.

I led design on Total Funds. Collapsed the 12 segments into four asset-type groups — Cards, Savings, Investments, outside-Sber — so the screen finally read as a composition of capital. Added leading buttons below the chart that opened next-product flows directly from the screen.

The donut form we landed on isn't statistically accurate and adjacent teams pushed back. We held the line on comprehension over precision, then partnered with Sber's gamification team on the spin: haptics, rolling numbers, color logic per category. ~88% of users actually spun it.

**Total Funds became Sber's 2nd-most-popular entry to the inner investment market; MAU 1.4M → 4.8M; $20M+ in invest-product cross-sell in 2021 only.**

images.alt: Total Funds widget inside the main Sber app surface
Five filter states of the asset donut: All, Cards, Savings, Investments, Outside Sber

sections.title: Financial Cushion
sections.description:
Financial Cushion is a savings-for-emergencies reserve — money set aside against income loss or sudden expense. The existing "Goals" product, where users used to set up such savings, had weak follow-through: most dropped off within one or two months.

The legacy flow asked for a target amount up front. People couldn't commit to an abstract number with no anchor in their own life and abandoned the goal soon after.

We built Cushion as a separate product, entered both from the bank's main screen and from a dedicated entry point on Total Funds. Instead of asking users to estimate a target, the flow auto-calculated it from their own monthly spending — replacing an abstract number with one anchored to real expense. Framed in human terms: "for what?", "how much do I actually need?". I led design as the team formed.

**Users who created Cushion through Total Funds kept accumulating for 6+ months at +81% the rate compare to users with ordinary Targets.**

images.alt: Financial Cushion setup flow with auto-calculated target

sections.title: Feature cut
sections.description:
After the rebuild we kept extending Total Funds — capital history, grouping by product type and currency, multi-angle filters. Each feature tested well; users in sessions praised the analytical depth.

Production told a different story. Engagement on parts of this layer stayed low even among high-income users — the same group most enthusiastic in tests. Testing measures comprehension; production measures want.

I ran a second round of research on real usage and owned the cut list with the PM. We cut the specific filter and grouping types nobody touched in production; capital history and the filters that mattered stayed. Foreground reduced to three core actions — balance, open-a-product, switch-grouping.

**Retention grown 2.6% → 5.3%; customer satisfaction lifted; inner invest market conversion kept rising.**

images.alt: Stripped-back Total Funds view with capital history graph
Dark mode treatment of the simplified Total Funds view
